channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: U.S. Might Be Close to a Deal With Iran
item.description: A reported memorandum would end the war, reopen the Strait of Hormuz, and lay the framework for future nuclear talks.

item.title: The BJP Scores Big in West Bengal
item.description: India’s ruling party got a boost from four state elections this week—and unseated a longtime rival.

item.title: China Tests a Rare Tool in Its Sanctions Arsenal
item.description: Ahead of the Trump-Xi summit, Beijing is pushing back on Washington.

item.title: Only one of Berkshire Hathaway and SoftBank can survive
item.description: The two represent competing visions of the future

item.title: Washington Is Still Chasing the Perfect War
item.description: Iran shows that the delusions that caused Iraq and Afghanistan persist.

item.title: The Iran War Is Eroding Israel’s Nuclear Ambiguity
item.description: Officially or not, Washington is now breaking the taboo and talking about its ally’s bombs.

item.title: Not all oil giants are prospering from the Iran war
item.description: Exxon and Chevron have benefited less than their European rivals

item.title: Why Did Beijing Kill a $2 Billion AI Deal?
item.description: The flow of global capital is hitting new walls.

item.title: Can a U.S.-Brokered State Budget Help Unite Libya?
item.description: Inside what analysts call a “pinky promise” between Libya’s rival factions.

item.title: Inside the Brussels deep state
item.description: The eurocratic guild at the heart of the EU is seeing its influence ebb

item.title: Iran’s missiles seek to drive a wedge between Gulf states
item.description: It is singling out the UAE for pain

item.title: The human genome encodes for a new category of molecule
item.description: They may be useful targets for future drugs

item.title: How worried should you be about hantavirus?
item.description: An outbreak on a cruise ship has authorities concerned

item.title: Is the Iran War Pushing Southeast Asia into China’s Arms?
item.description: U.S. guarantees have not shielded Indonesia, the Philippines, and Vietnam from economic shock.

item.title: The Iran Standoff and the Future of Oil
item.description: The Iranians know that the usual US endgame in a war of choice is to walk away from defeat, take the humiliation, and move on. The most likely outcome in the current confrontation, then, is that Iran will prevail, the oil price will fall, and the US private-equity interests that have profited from the war will go bust.

item.title: City parenting has become a financial flex
item.description: The wealthiest neighbourhoods are defying suburbanisation

item.title: How Should Europe Respond to the Energy Shock?
item.description: So far, European governments’ response to the Iran energy shock has missed the mark. Rather than subsidizing energy consumption by reducing fuel taxes, they should be pushing forward with a system that discourages emissions, incentivizes the adoption of renewables, and generates public revenues.

item.title: Friedrich Merz can’t go on like this
item.description: His one-year-old government looks exhausted, and voters are tiring rapidly

item.title: America is massing troops near Taiwan to deter troublemaking by China
item.description: The timing of annual exercises is set by weather patterns favourable to any possible invasion

item.title: The United States’ Korea Strategy Is Working Against Itself
item.description: By pulling Seoul into its rivalry with Beijing, Washington is undermining the alliance’s core mission.

item.title: “Midwest Nice” is no match for presidential petty
item.description: In Indiana, Donald Trump takes his revenge

item.title: Putin Should Be Challenged to Restore Local Democracy in Russia
item.description: Ever since the American and French Revolutions, which took markedly different paths, democracy has grown most steadily when it has been rooted in local politics. Those who want to see a genuine democracy develop in Russia should begin there.

item.title: China and Japan Are Entering a More Dangerous Phase of Rivalry
item.description: Neither China nor Japan wants war. The danger is that each side increasingly believes the other is preparing for one.

item.title: Iraq’s Continuing Struggle for Sovereignty
item.description: Amid a regional war, political drama and drone strikes are testing Baghdad’s power.

item.title: Wanted: a new tech-industry writer
item.description: An opportunity to join the staff of The Economist

item.title: The US Is Running Dangerously Low on China Expertise
item.description: Insights from Rosie Levine.

item.title: Southeast Asia’s Scam Compounds: How the Law Fails Survivors
item.description: Many of the people who send fraudulent messages or manage fake investment accounts are victims of human trafficking and forced criminality.

item.title: A Year After Operation Sindoor: Rising Risks and Deepening Instability
item.description: The next India-Pakistan crisis will be shaped by compressed timelines, more domestic pressure, weaker external constraints, and the perception that escalation can be controlled.

item.title: 10 Years After the Arbitration Ruling, Might Makes Right in the South China Sea
item.description: Beijing will disregard international law when in its own interest, forcing other countries to make difficult decisions about their own compliance.

item.title: My Brother’s 16-Year Sentence Was Not Enough for Uzbekistan. Now They Want Him to Disappear.
item.description: Dauletmurat Tajimuratov was sentenced to 16 years in prison in 2023. Last week 2.5 years were added to his sentence.

item.title: Fiscal Discipline Requires More than Rules
item.description: While there is no easy fix for rising government debt levels, research and experience have shown that a well-designed fiscal framework can tilt policy choices toward financial discipline. Two areas, in particular, could be improved upon.

item.title: What Critics Get Wrong About China’s Digital Silk Road
item.description: The DSR continues to thrive largely because recipient governments see it as an attractive alternative.

item.title: Analysing Africa newsletter: Inside a counter-terrorism bootcamp
item.description: Tom Gardner, our Africa correspondent, watches an American-led effort to fight jihadism

item.title: A Generation Rethinking Marriage Amid Rising Family Strain in Uzbekistan
item.description: Behind steady population growth, shifting expectations and rising domestic tensions are transforming family life.

item.title: Will AI Democratize Skills?
item.description: While AI does not eliminate the role of human capital in performance or equalize fundamental abilities, new research finds that it relaxes execution constraints that are more binding for less-educated individuals. But firms and governments must ensure that the workers who stand to benefit the most from these tools can access them.

item.title: Jerome Powell’s Mixed Legacy
item.description: By remaining on the US Federal Reserve board of governors after the end of his term as chair, Jerome Powell can keep the focus on his widely celebrated role as the “defender of the Fed.” But if that allows him to sideline substantive criticism of Fed policy during his tenure, the institution may suffer for it.

item.title: Iran’s Survival Is Not Victory
item.description: Tehran’s latest rhetoric is reframing its abdication as a self-respecting state.

item.title: Iran Has All the Hallmarks of a Forever War
item.description: There is a way out of this mess—but not right away.

item.title: ASEAN’s Angry Summit
item.description: Frustration bubbles with the United States ahead of the leaders’ gathering in Cebu.

item.title: The Lessons of the Long Confucian Peace
item.description: Can ideology prevent war in East Asia?

item.title: Iran’s New Oil Weapon
item.description: How America can protect itself—and the global economy.

item.title: Terror Alert in Bangladesh: Between Denial and Reality
item.description: Ignoring quiet militant activity like online recruitment or fundraising is just as dangerous as exaggerating the militant threat for political reasons.

item.title: Why the ‘Control Rod’ Wouldn’t Budge: 20 Years of OPCON Transfer Debates
item.description: The difficulty and delay of OPCON transfer has not been because either side is unwilling. The question is no longer whether to transfer but how – and how well.
